title: Theorem 2.6: irreducibility $\Rightarrow$ existence of unitaries $u_1,u_2\in M_1$, $u_3\in M_2$ in free position over $B$

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Location:** Proof of Theorem 2.6, the sentence "Since $\Gamma$ is irreducible, either $M_1$ must contain two unitaries $u_1,u_2$ ... (otherwise ...)".

For the AFP decomposition $M=\Gamma(\mathcal G\setminus\{v\})\ast_{\Gamma(\mathrm{link}(v))}\Gamma(\mathrm{star}(v))$ with $M_1=\Gamma(\mathcal G\setminus\{v\})$, $B=\Gamma(\mathrm{link}(v))$, $M_2=\Gamma(\mathrm{star}(v))$, Lemma 2.5 needs $u_1,u_2\in\mathcal U(M_1)$ and $u_3\in\mathcal U(M_2)$ with $E_B(u_1)=E_B(u_2)=E_B(u_3)=E_B(u_1^*u_2)=0$.

The written case analysis is terse and appears to contain a typo ("$(u,u')\in\mathcal V(\mathcal G)$" should presumably be an edge $(u,u')\in\mathcal E$). It must be made rigorous:
- Construct $u_3$: a trace-zero unitary at vertex $v$ lies in $\mathrm{star}(v)$ but not $\mathrm{link}(v)$, so $E_B(u_3)=0$. (Uses the hypothesis that each $M_w$ has a trace-zero unitary.)
- Construct $u_1,u_2\in M_1$ from trace-zero vertex unitaries at vertices outside $\mathrm{star}(v)$; the conditions $E_B(u_1)=E_B(u_2)=E_B(u_1^*u_2)=0$ require either two **non-adjacent** such vertices (free independence) or careful handling when only one vertex lies outside $\mathrm{star}(v)$.
- Show the "only one vertex outside $\mathrm{star}(v)$" + "that vertex adjacent to ..." sub-case contradicts irreducibility (using $|\mathcal V|\ge3$).

Each branch must be tied precisely to the stated definition of irreducibility.
